title: Koxia and Game
description: Koxia and Mahiru are playing a game with three arrays $$$a$$$, $$$b$$$, and $$$c$$$ of length $$$n$$$. Each element of $$$a$$$, $$$b$$$ and $$$c$$$ is an integer between $$$1$$$ and $$$n$$$ inclusive. The game consists of $$$n$$$ rounds. In the $$$i$$$-th round, they perform the following moves: Let $$$S$$$ be the multiset $$$\{a_i, b_i, c_i\}$$$. Koxia removes one element from the multiset $$$S$$$ by her choice. Mahiru chooses one integer from the two remaining in the multiset $$$S$$$. Let $$$d_i$$$ be the integer Mahiru chose in the $$$i$$$-th round. If $$$d$$$ is a permutation$$$^\dagger$$$, Koxia wins. Otherwise, Mahiru wins. Currently, only the arrays $$$a$$$ and $$$b$$$ have been chosen. As an avid supporter of Koxia, you want to choose an array $$$c$$$ such that Koxia will win. Count the number of such $$$c$$$, modulo $$$998\,244\,353$$$. Note that Koxia and Mahiru both play optimally. $$$^\dagger$$$ A permutation of length $$$n$$$ is an array consisting of $$$n$$$ distinct integers from $$$1$$$ to $$$n$$$ in arbitrary order. For example, $$$[2,3,1,5,4]$$$ is a permutation, but $$$[1,2,2]$$$ is not a permutation ($$$2$$$ appears twice in the array), and $$$[1,3,4]$$$ is also not a permutation ($$$n=3$$$ but there is $$$4$$$ in the array).
input_spec: Each test consists of multiple test cases. The first line contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \leq t \leq 2 \cdot 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases. The description of test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$1 \leq n \leq {10}^5$$$) — the size of the arrays. The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \leq a_i \leq n$$$). The third line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$b_1, b_2, \dots, b_n$$$ ($$$1 \leq b_i \leq n$$$). It is guaranteed that the sum of $$$n$$$ over all test cases does not exceed $$${10}^5$$$.
output_spec: Output a single integer — the number of $$$c$$$ makes Koxia win, modulo $$$998\,244\,353$$$.

routes.name: Graph modeling with DSU
routes.description: Reduce the game to selecting p_i from {a_i, b_i} to form a permutation, model this as a graph where each index i is an edge between a_i and b_i, analyze connected components using DSU, and count valid arrays c by multiplying contributions per component.

observations.title: Koxia must eliminate choice: the two remaining elements must be equal
observations.description: Since Mahiru plays optimally and wants to prevent d from being a permutation, Koxia can only guarantee a specific d_i value if the two elements she leaves in S are identical. Working backwards from round n, each d_i is uniquely forced. Therefore Koxia wins if and only if for every round i, c_i can be chosen so that S = {a_i, b_i, c_i} contains a duplicate, letting Koxia remove the odd one out.

hints.nudge: Think about what happens in the last round. If Mahiru has two distinct values to choose from, can she always spoil the permutation?
hints.question: If Koxia leaves two different values for Mahiru in round n, Mahiru picks whichever ruins the permutation. What must Koxia do to guarantee d_n?
hints.formal: In each round, Koxia must make the two remaining elements equal so Mahiru has no real choice. This means c_i must equal either a_i or b_i, and d_i is the other value. Equivalently, we must pick p_i from {a_i, b_i} for each i such that p forms a permutation of [1..n].

observations.title: Reduction to choosing p_i from {a_i, b_i} to form a permutation
observations.description: By the forced-choice argument, Koxia wins iff we can build an array p where p_i is either a_i or b_i, and p is a permutation of {1,...,n}. Once p is determined, we set c_i = p_i (the value Koxia wants Mahiru to pick), and Koxia removes the third element. The number of winning c arrays equals the number of such permutations p, multiplied by any freedom in the c_i values themselves.

hints.nudge: Given that d_i is forced, what does c_i need to be? And what happens to the removed element -- does it matter what it is?
hints.question: If p_i is the value Mahiru is forced to pick, what must c_i equal? And for the element Koxia removes, does the problem constrain it beyond being in [1,n]?
hints.formal: Set c_i = p_i so two elements in {a_i, b_i, c_i} are equal (c_i matches one of them). Koxia removes the non-matching one, Mahiru picks p_i. When a_i = b_i, then p_i = a_i is forced and c_i can be any value in [1,n] -- but actually c_i must equal a_i = b_i for the duplicate condition, so c_i is free to be anything since the multiset already has a duplicate.

observations.title: Model as a graph: each index i is an edge between nodes a_i and b_i
observations.description: Build a graph on nodes {1,...,n} where for each index i, we add an edge between a_i and b_i. Choosing p_i = a_i means node a_i is 'used' by index i; choosing p_i = b_i means node b_i is used. For p to be a permutation, each node must be used exactly once. This is equivalent to finding a perfect matching-like orientation of the edges in this graph.

hints.nudge: Each index i gives a choice between two values. This sounds like edges in a graph connecting two candidate values. What does the permutation condition mean on this graph?
hints.question: If you create a graph where index i is an edge (a_i, b_i), what must be true about each node's degree and the selection for p to be a permutation?
hints.formal: Build graph G on vertex set {1,...,n} with n edges (one per index). For p to be a permutation, each vertex v must be chosen as p_i by exactly one edge incident to v. Each connected component with k vertices must have exactly k edges (one cycle), forming a unicyclic graph.

observations.title: Each connected component must be unicyclic (exactly k vertices and k edges)
observations.description: A connected component with k vertices and fewer than k edges is a tree (k-1 edges), which cannot assign each vertex exactly once since there aren't enough edges. More than k edges means some vertex must be assigned twice. So each component must have exactly k vertices and k edges, meaning it contains exactly one cycle. If any component violates this, the answer is 0.

hints.nudge: In a connected graph with k vertices, how many edges do you need for each vertex to be 'covered' exactly once by an edge assignment?
hints.question: A tree on k vertices has k-1 edges. Can you orient k-1 edges to cover all k vertices? What structure has exactly k edges on k vertices?
hints.formal: A connected component on k vertices needs exactly k edges so that each vertex is the chosen endpoint of exactly one edge. This means k vertices, k edges, so exactly one cycle. A component with fewer edges (tree) cannot cover all vertices; more edges makes it impossible. Use DSU: when merging, track if adding an edge creates a second cycle (answer becomes 0).

observations.title: The cycle determines the assignment; each unicyclic component contributes a factor
observations.description: In a unicyclic component, the edges on the cycle can be oriented in exactly 2 ways (clockwise or counterclockwise), each giving a valid assignment. The tree edges hanging off the cycle are then forced. However, if any edge on the cycle has a_i = b_i (a self-loop), that edge's orientation is forced, so the component contributes only 1 way instead of 2. When a_i = b_i for an index i, c_i can be any value in [1,n], contributing a factor of n to the count for each such index.

hints.nudge: Once you know the cycle in a component, how many ways can you orient the cycle edges? What about the tree edges?
hints.question: A cycle of length L can be traversed in 2 directions. Does each direction give a valid assignment? And when a_i = b_i (a self-loop), how does that affect the cycle?
hints.formal: For each unicyclic component: the cycle can be oriented in 2 ways, giving 2 valid permutation-assignments. Tree edges off the cycle are forced. If the cycle contains a self-loop (a_i = b_i), orientation is forced, contributing factor 1. For each self-loop index, c_i is free in [1,n], contributing a multiplicative factor of n. Total answer = product over all components of their contribution, times n^(number of self-loop indices), modulo 998244353.

observations.title: Implement with DSU to detect components and count in O(n alpha(n))
observations.description: Use a Disjoint Set Union (DSU) structure. Process each index i: if a_i and b_i are already in the same component, this edge closes a cycle. If the component already had a cycle, a second cycle makes the answer 0. Track per-component whether it has a cycle and whether that cycle includes a self-loop. The final answer is the product of each component's contribution (2 if the cycle has no self-loop, 1 if it does) times n raised to the number of self-loops, all modulo 998244353.

hints.nudge: You need to efficiently track connected components and detect cycles. What data structure is ideal for incremental edge additions with connectivity queries?
hints.question: When you process edge (a_i, b_i) and find a_i and b_i are already connected, what does that mean? How do you track the number of cycles per component with DSU?
hints.formal: Initialize DSU on {1,...,n}. For each i: if find(a_i) == find(b_i), this is the cycle-closing edge -- if the component already has a cycle, output 0. Otherwise mark it as having a cycle. If a_i == b_i, mark the component's cycle as containing a self-loop and multiply the answer by n (for the free c_i). Finally, answer = product over components of (1 if self-loop cycle, 2 otherwise), times n^(self-loop count), mod 998244353.
